title: The Clue That Was Missed
concept: Cliffhanger: the fix for XOR was hiding in plain sight — stacking multiple perceptrons into layers instead of relying on just one — setting up the breakthrough in the next unit.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What era followed the perceptron's proven limits and unmet AI promises?
exercises.explain: Right — funding and confidence collapsed for over a decade.

cards.title: The clue nobody chased
cards.body: Here's a twist: the fix for the XOR problem wasn't some brand-new invention waiting to be discovered decades later. A version of the idea was floating around even back in Minsky and Papert's own book. It just didn't get chased down — yet.

cards.title: One line isn't the only option
cards.body: A single perceptron can only draw one straight line. But what if, instead of relying on one perceptron alone, you took the output of a couple of perceptrons and fed it into another perceptron? Suddenly you're not limited to one straight cut anymore.

cards.title: Stacking, not settling
cards.body: Think of building a sandwich: one slice of bread alone doesn't make a sandwich. But stack a few layers — bread, filling, more bread — and you get something a single slice never could. Neural networks work the same way: stack simple perceptron "layers" and the whole becomes more capable than any one piece.

cards.title: A hint hiding in the AND/OR toolkit
cards.body: You already know a single perceptron CAN learn AND and a single perceptron CAN learn OR. What if one perceptron's job was to check something with AND, another checked something with OR, and a THIRD perceptron combined their two answers? That chain of perceptrons is exactly the shape of the eventual solution.

cards.title: It wasn't a total mystery
cards.body: It's easy to imagine the AI Winter as a total dead end where nobody had any ideas. Not quite true — the general concept of connecting layers of perceptrons together was already known. What was missing wasn't the idea itself, but a reliable, efficient way to actually train a stack of them.

cards.title: One perceptron vs. a stack
cards.body: The shape of the fix, side by side:

right.text:
Multiple lines combined
Can carve out ANY shape

exercises.prompt: What idea hints at a fix for the XOR problem?

options.text: Combining the outputs of multiple perceptrons together, instead of relying on just one
Training a single perceptron for a much longer time
Removing the false outputs from the truth table

exercises.explain: Stacking perceptrons together — using one perceptron's output as another's input — is the shape of the real fix.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The idea of connecting layers of perceptrons together was already known during the AI Winter — what was missing was an efficient way to train them.
exercises.explain: True! The concept existed; the missing piece was a practical training method — which is exactly where the story picks up next.

exercises.sentence: Instead of one straight line, ___ perceptrons together lets a network carve out far more complex shapes.
exercises.explain: Stacking (layering) perceptrons is the key idea that eventually broke through the XOR wall.

exercises.prompt: Which setup could realistically separate XOR's true points from its false points?

options.text: A single perceptron drawing one straight line
Multiple perceptrons whose outputs feed into another perceptron
A perceptron with a bigger truth table

exercises.explain: Combining perceptrons' outputs is the missing ingredient — one line still can't do it alone.

exercises.prompt: You've now seen: (1) XOR needs two dividing lines, not one, (2) Minsky and Papert proved a SINGLE perceptron can never do that, and (3) the concept of chaining perceptrons together already existed even back then. Based on everything in this unit, what's the most likely direction the field had to go to finally crack XOR?

options.text: Arrange multiple perceptrons into connected layers, and find a way to train the whole stack together
Abandon perceptrons entirely and switch to a completely different kind of computer
Wait for someone to redefine what 'true' and 'false' mean
Give up on logic gates and only use regression instead

exercises.explain: You called it! Layering perceptrons — building what we now call a multi-layer neural network — was the path forward. But an idea isn't a solution until someone finds how to TRAIN it. That's exactly where the next chapter of this story begins.
